attributes.stem:
A 21-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with painful ulcers on her trunk and extremities that have developed over two weeks. She was recently diagnosed with acute myeloid leukaemia, for which she has completed one cycle of chemotherapy. She takes no other regular medications. She appears unwell with a pulse rate of 110/min, blood pressure 115/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C.

On examination, there are multiple tender, punched-out ulcers with raised erythematous borders distributed across her trunk and limbs. Some lesions show central necrosis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This immunocompromised patient presents with painful, ulcerated skin lesions in the context of fever and pancytopenia. The presentation is concerning for cutaneous cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection, which typically manifests as ulcerated lesions in immunocompromised hosts, particularly those with haematological malignancies or HIV. Perianal ulcerations are also common in these cases. Whilst CMV typically presents with mild disease of fever or fatigue, it is important to know of the complications of CMV as well.

Skin biopsy with immunohistochemistry is the gold standard for diagnosing cutaneous CMV infection. The biopsy will show characteristic viral cytopathic effects including enlarged cells with intranuclear inclusions ('owl's eye' appearance), and immunohistochemistry can specifically identify CMV antigens.

A viral polymerase chain reaction swab of the lesions would be less helpful as it may not distinguish between colonisation and true infection. Additionally, the depth of tissue invasion, which is important for diagnosis, cannot be assessed.

An antinuclear antibody test would be useful if vasculitis was suspected, but the acute presentation and patient's immunocompromised status make an autoimmune condition less likely.

A Tzanck smear is useful for diagnosing herpes simplex virus or varicella zoster virus infections but cannot identify CMV infection. The morphology of the lesions is also not typical for these herpesvirus infections.

An anticardiolipin antibody test would be appropriate if antiphospholipid syndrome was suspected, but the acute onset and immunocompromised state make this diagnosis unlikely. Additionally, antiphospholipid syndrome typically causes livedo reticularis rather than ulcerated lesions.

Once cutaneous CMV is diagnosed, systemic antiviral therapy with ganciclovir or valganciclovir is indicated, as cutaneous involvement represents disseminated disease. The presence of cutaneous CMV is associated with significant morbidity and mortality in immunocompromised patients if left untreated.
learningPoints: Cutaneous cytomegalovirus typically presents as ulcerated lesions in immunocompromised patients.
Biopsy with immunohistochemistry is required for definitive diagnosis of cutaneous cytomegalovirus.
The presence of cutaneous cytomegalovirus indicates systemic infection requiring antiviral therapy.
references: Narayan Prasad, Manoj Jain, Amit Gupta, Raj K. Sharma, Vinita Agarwal, An unusual case of CMV cutaneous ulcers in a renal transplant recipient and review of literature.  Nephrology Dialysis Transplantation. 2010: 3 (4).  Available at: https://academic.oup.com/ckj/article/3/4/379/558212?login=false

Colsky A. et al. Diagnosis and treatment of a case of cutaneous cytomegalovirus infection with a dramatic clinical presentation. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology.  1998: 38 (2).  Available at: https://www.jaad.org/article/S0190-9622(98)70581-X/fulltext
